title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] SIMONA MILANOVIC: Hi, everyone,
and welcome to our session on how to build more accessible
UIs with Jetpack Compose. My name is Simona Milanovic. I'm an Android Developer
Relations Engineer at Google. MELBA NUZEN: And I'm Melba
Nuzen, a Software Engineer working on Jetpack Compose. SIMONA MILANOVIC: Developing
with accessibility in mind means making your apps usable
by even more people, including those with accessibility
needs who may use Android devices in different ways. So, in this talk,
we'll focus on how to leverage powerful declarative
Compose APIs and tools to build apps that are more
inclusive and accessible. We'll cover practical
steps and concepts in supporting
accessibility with Compose, starting with how to classify
composables and modify them with the appropriate
APIs and semantics. We'll also show how to inspect
the composable semantics with tools that can
be used for debugging unexpected behaviors
when used with Android's assistive technologies. Finally, we'll cover how to
detect common accessibility issues and automate some
aspects of testing with Compose accessibility checks. By the end, you'll have the
right tools and techniques to create more
delightful and inclusive experiences for more users. For updates in
Android accessibility, check out the linked I/O talk. As the first step, it's helpful
to classify your component, as this also impacts the
level of modification of its accessibility
information. A ready-made material
component will differ in potential adjustments
from a custom component. From an accessibility
perspective, you can put composables into
roughly three categories, material components, Compose
Foundation and UI APIs, and custom components. These range from none to
minimal semantic modifications for material to potentially
larger structural changes for more custom components. This classification helps
you choose and integrate more information into the UI
elements earlier in the app development. This information on
the meaning and role
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
for material to potentially
larger structural changes for more custom components. This classification helps
you choose and integrate more information into the UI
elements earlier in the app development. This information on
the meaning and role of a component that is sent
to accessibility services is called semantics. For example, a camera icon
might be visually just an image, but the semantic meaning
could be take a photo. To learn more about
accessibility basics ahead of this talk, check out the
linked documentation and video. By combining the right
semantics with Compose APIs, you provide as much information
as possible about your component to accessibility services,
which then decide how and what to represent
to the user. MELBA NUZEN: Let's
look at each category, starting with
material components. These pre-built UI elements
include built-in semantics that follow their specific
role and function, meaning that most
accessibility support is provided for you with little
or no additional work. For example, a material
button might consist of a child icon and some text. Instead of treating the
children as individuals, a material button merges its
children's semantics by default so that accessibility services
can treat them as one entity. Merging is a powerful
concept in semantics and widely used,
as we'll see later. But in general,
material components require little
adjustment in order to be more accessible when used
for their intended purpose. Similarly, in Compose
UI and Foundation APIs, many accessible practices
are also followed by default. Using the right APIs
for the right purpose usually means you get
predefined semantics that cover standard use cases,
but you should double check that these
defaults fit your needs. If it isn't enough
for some cases, Compose also provides
the tools to adjust for more specific requirements. Most semantic
properties are a way to simply provide
additional information. Setting something like a
content description or a state
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
Compose also provides
the tools to adjust for more specific requirements. Most semantic
properties are a way to simply provide
additional information. Setting something like a
content description or a state description provides more
metadata about a UI component to services that
consume semantics. But some semantics require
more careful consideration, since they can influence how
the structure of a screen is interpreted, rather
than only provide additional helpful information. Two, Compose APIs
are specifically meant to support
accessibility traversal order. When using composables
with predefined semantics, there could be a need to
modify their traversal order, or the order in which
accessibility services navigate through UI elements. By default, Compose arranges
elements in expected reading order, usually top to
bottom, then left to right. While this normally
creates a logical order, there are some scenarios where
top to bottom, left to right is not sufficient and Compose
might need additional hints. In these cases, you can use
Compose's isTraversalGroup and traversalIndex APIs. IsTraversalGroup marks
logical semantic groups that need customization,
while traversalIndex adjusts the order of individual
elements within those groups. Let's look closer at
the clock face example. The UI consists of
a circular layout where the numbers of
the clock are placed, and some clock text containing
the actual number values. To influence the
default traversal order, we set isTraversalGroup equals
true on the surrounding parent, in this case, the
circular layout. Then we assign increasing
traversalIndex values to each child
element so that they can be numbered numerically. To influence traversal
behavior, this API must be set on a
component that will be selectable by
accessibility services. In this case, we set the
index on the text itself rather than the
containing box parent. With those changes, we've
adjusted our traversal order to be more logical for
accessibility services.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
be selectable by
accessibility services. In this case, we set the
index on the text itself rather than the
containing box parent. With those changes, we've
adjusted our traversal order to be more logical for
accessibility services. As you apply these APIs, here
are some things to keep in mind. IsTraversalGroup
equals true should be set on the parent containing
the grouped elements. TraversalIndex should be set
on a child component that contains semantics and will be
used by accessibility services. A no semantics are
unnecessarily merged, as this may affect which
components traversal indices are applied to. SIMONA MILANOVIC:
Foundation APIs that make your composable
clickable, toggleable, or overall interactable,
use the same merging strategies as the
material button, which merges its children
semantics by default. Using the clickable
modifier makes that composable merge
its descendant semantics into a single entity, which
then gets represented together by the accessibility services,
along with the corresponding action representation. You can also set the on-click
label in the parent clickable to provide additional
information to accessibility
services, and offer a more polished representation. For this modifier
and its click label, using TalkBack as an example,
it would provide an action hint of double tap to
open this article, rather than the generic default
of double tap to activate. This feedback changes
depending on the action type. So, a long click would provide
a TalkBack hint of double tap and hold to, usually
followed by a verb label. In cases where you
don't have direct access to a clickable
modifier, for example, if it's set somewhere
in a lower nested layer, but still want to change
the default action label, you can split setting
the clickable action from modifying its semantics. You can do so using modifier
semantics and its on-click label to modify the action
representation there. In this case, you don't need
to pass the click action twice,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
you can split setting
the clickable action from modifying its semantics. You can do so using modifier
semantics and its on-click label to modify the action
representation there. In this case, you don't need
to pass the click action twice, as certain Compose APIs, like
clickable, handle this for you and pass it down to their
intrinsic clickable semantics. You might run into
scenarios where you expect children's semantics
to be merged into the parent one, but that doesn't happen. In this example, we
have a clickable parent with three children
elements, and we might expect it merges all of them. However, some composables
would not automatically merge under a parent by design. A parent cannot merge its
children when the children are also merging, either from
explicitly setting merge descendants to true, or by
being components which merge themselves, like
buttons or clickables. Knowing how certain APIs
merge or define merging can help you debug
unexpected behaviors. Custom actions can sometimes
help improve the user experience of accessibility services. Complex touchscreen
gestures, like swipe to dismiss or drag-and-drop,
can be challenging for users with accessibility needs. So custom actions can be
used as a more accessible representation. For example, to make the
swipe to dismiss gesture more accessible, you can
link it to a custom action, passing the dismissal
action and label there. An accessibility
service, like TalkBack, would then highlight
the component and hint that there are more
actions available in its menu, representing the swipe
to dismiss action there. This only changes
the swipe to dismiss for accessibility services. You can still use the
original swipe gesture. In long lists with items that
have multiple available actions, it might be tedious
for users to iterate through each action for every
single item individually. To improve the navigation
experience, which is especially helpful for
interaction-based assistive technologies, like Switch
access or Voice access,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
through each action for every
single item individually. To improve the navigation
experience, which is especially helpful for
interaction-based assistive technologies, like Switch
access or Voice access, you can use custom
actions on the parent item and move its nested actions
out of the individual traversal and into a separate action menu. Using Switch access
as an example, its menu opens on
selecting the parent item, offering the available
actions there. However, make sure
to always clear the original semantics
of items which actions you're moving elsewhere. We'll see more on this later. MELBA NUZEN: So
far we've covered composables with some
predefined semantics, but what if you're building
your own custom component? As a general rule
of thumb, it can be helpful to see semantics
of similar components in the material or
other Compose libraries. But another tool we can use
is manual semantic merging. If your custom component
consists of multiple elements, you can manually join
their semantic information to treat the component
as one entity. This is done by setting
modifier.semantics, merge descendants equals true. Under the hood,
merging components, like a material button
and modifier.clickable or modifier.toggleable, set
this flag to true as well. Setting this API collects
and combines data from non-merging
children, as merging won't apply to any children
that are merging themselves. Let's look at an example of
a custom avatar component. We have three parts, an avatar
image, a status overlay, and a profile name overlay. As is, accessibility services
see each part separately. But it would make more sense
to consider the entire avatar component together, rather than
treat each element individually. To do this, we've
set merge descendants equals true on the box
containing all three elements. Then the component is
grouped accordingly. But what about times
where we don't need to send semantic information? In some scenarios,
certain elements should not be sent to
accessibility services,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
equals true on the box
containing all three elements. Then the component is
grouped accordingly. But what about times
where we don't need to send semantic information? In some scenarios,
certain elements should not be sent to
accessibility services, perhaps because their
additional information is redundant for accessibility or
because they are purely visually decorative and non-interactive. In these cases, you
can hide elements with the hideFromAccessibility
API, introduced in Compose version 1.8. In the following examples,
we see decorative components that can be hidden. A redundant watermark that
spans a box and a character used to decoratively
separate information. Using hideFromAccessibility here
ensures that these components are hidden from
accessibility services, but still retain their
semantics for other purposes, like testing. However, if semantic
information must be completely cleared
or overwritten, then a more powerful API to
use is clearAndSetSemantics. When a component needs its own
and its descendant semantics cleared, use this API
with an empty lambda. Note that when clearing
with an empty lambda, the cleared semantics
are not sent to any consumer, such as
accessibility, autofill, or testing. When semantics must
be overwritten, include your new content
inside the lambda. When overriding content with
new semantics inside the lambda, the new semantics replace
all previous semantics of the element and
its descendants. Let's look at a
favorite toggle example, represented by an interactable
row with an icon and some text. Although the icon and text
have some semantic information, together, they do not
indicate that this component is a toggleable. Merging alone is not
sufficient, since we must also set the toggle ability, as well
as role and state semantics, so that component status
and associated action may be available. For example, TalkBack would
announce double tap to toggle rather than double
tap to activate. By clearing the
original semantics and setting new, more
descriptive ones,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
so that component status
and associated action may be available. For example, TalkBack would
announce double tap to toggle rather than double
tap to activate. By clearing the
original semantics and setting new, more
descriptive ones, accessibility
services can now see that this is a
toggleable component that can alternate state. Some things to remember. Since services receive
no semantic information when this API is
set, it's better to be conservative
about its use. Semantic information
can potentially be used by AI agents
and similar services to understand the screen,
and thus should only be cleared when necessary. Custom semantics may be
set within the API lambda. And lastly, ordering of
the modifiers matters. This API clears
all semantics that are after where it is
applied, regardless of other merging strategies. Note that in our sample,
since clearance at semantics is set after the
clickable modifier, the clickable semantics
are not cleared. We've covered quite a
few APIs and concepts. To recap, when content
is not intended to be used by
accessibility services, use hideFromAccessibility
when it is possibly decorative
or redundant, but still must be tested. Use clearAndSetSemantics
with an empty lambda when parent and
children semantics need to be cleared for all services. Use clearAndSetSemantics
with content inside a lambda when a component's semantics
need to be manually set. And when content should
be treated as one entity with all children
information needed to be considered complete, use
merge semantic descendants. SIMONA MILANOVIC: To
specify and signal the content type of
your custom component, you can use a
variety of semantics. Content type semantics support
the main semantic information, for example, the text
of a text composable, and help accessibility
services fine tune how the component is
represented, announced, or interacted with. For instance, if your component
is an alert or a pop-up, like a Snackbar, you
might want to signal to accessibility services that
a new structure or updates
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
tune how the component is
represented, announced, or interacted with. For instance, if your component
is an alert or a pop-up, like a Snackbar, you
might want to signal to accessibility services that
a new structure or updates to content should be
conveyed to users. Alert-like components can be
marked with the LiveRegion semantics. This allows
accessibility services to automatically
notify of changes to its component
or the children. Polite mode should be
used in most cases, where users' attention should be drawn
to alerts or important changing content on screen. Assertive mode should
only be used sparingly to avoid disruptive
feedback, and should be reserved for
when it's crucial that users are aware of
time sensitive content. LiveRegions should
not be used on content that updates frequently,
such as countdown timers, to avoid overwhelming
users with feedback. Window-like custom components,
similar to modal bottom sheet, need additional signals
to differentiate them from surrounding content. For this, you can use
the pane title semantics so that relevant window or
pane changes are represented appropriately by
accessibility services, along with its main
semantic information. Screens with a lot of
textual information, like long articles
or news, are usually divided into different
subsections by using headings. Some accessibility
services, like TalkBack, allow for easier
navigation directly between sections or headings. To enable this, indicate that
your component is a heading by defining its semantics. For other content types, such
as error-like components, you might want to expand on
the main semantic information for users with
accessibility needs. When defining the
error states, you can inform the accessibility
services of its error semantics and provide expanded
error messaging. In this example, TalkBack
would hint the main error text, followed by additional
expanded messaging. For custom components
that track progress, you might want to notify
users of its progress changes,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
and provide expanded
error messaging. In this example, TalkBack
would hint the main error text, followed by additional
expanded messaging. For custom components
that track progress, you might want to notify
users of its progress changes, like the current progress
value, its range, and step size. You can do so with
progressBarRangeInfo. This ensures that
accessibility services are aware of progress
changes and can update users accordingly. Different assistive
technologies may have unique ways of
hinting, increasing, and decreasing progression. In custom lists and
grids with many items, it might be helpful for
accessibility services to also have more
detailed information, like the total number
of items and indices, in addition to their main
semantic information by using collection info and collection
item info on the list and items respectively, and
TalkBack as an example. In this list, the
service announces the total number of items, each
item's textual information, and its item's
index out of total. There are a number of
tools that can help quickly inspect the content from an
accessibility point of view. Using the Android
Accessibility Suite, which includes Accessibility
Menu, Select to Speak, and others, provides
insight into how your app works for users
of these technologies. Testing with these
assistive technologies is highly recommended
as the best way to understand the
experience of app users with accessibility needs. Layout Inspector allows
you to inspect and debug semantics of
composables and helps identify any missing or
incorrect information. The accessibility scanner
app scans your screen and provides suggestions to
improve its accessibility by identifying some
common pitfalls. MELBA NUZEN: Between Compose,
the semantic system, and Android Accessibility
Services, you might run into unexpected
accessibility behaviors that are difficult to trace. But there's a good chance
that semantic properties can help you understand
why your components are behaving the way they are.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
Accessibility
Services, you might run into unexpected
accessibility behaviors that are difficult to trace. But there's a good chance
that semantic properties can help you understand
why your components are behaving the way they are. You can debug with a
tool of your choice, from Layout Inspector
in Android Studio, to TreeDebug in TalkBack
developer settings, to composeTestRule.printToLog. These tools provide information
about nodes and their properties that are exposed to
accessibility services by Compose. As an example, let's take
a look at Layout Inspector. What if you had a screen
with three elements, but with accessibility
services on, the first one wasn't
being selected and the second one didn't have
any action feedback associated with it? We can examine the
semantic properties to see if there's anything suspicious. The component tree
in Layout Inspector contains information
about an element's bounds, parameters, and
semantic properties. In our tree, we see
all three elements, but the first one has a hide
from accessibility applied. That can help us debug why
it isn't being selected. Maybe the element is
marked as hidden somewhere in the semantics
tree, or Compose considers it obscured because
of some decorative overlay. The second element
has a focus property, but no on-click like
the previous element. So this element is probably
missing a clickable modifier somewhere, which is why
an accessibility service, like TalkBack, may not announce
an action signal to the user, like double tap to activate. If we look at the
third element, it has all the properties we want. It is focusable,
has an on-click, and other additional
semantics are applied, which is why it's
interpreted as expected. In this way, debugging
tools can help investigate why certain announcements
or selections aren't happening with your
accessibility services. Much of what we covered today
can be manually verified, for example, by turning
an accessibility service, like Switch Access,
on and checking that all works as expected.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
happening with your
accessibility services. Much of what we covered today
can be manually verified, for example, by turning
an accessibility service, like Switch Access,
on and checking that all works as expected. However, in conjunction
with manual verification, you should also use
automated testing to flag any potential issues that
could impact user experiences. Existing testing
APIs in Compose allow you to write
automated tests that interact with semantic
elements, and to assert properties defined in your UI. But you can now use the
Accessibility Test Framework, the same framework underlying
Accessibility Scanner and Accessibility
Checks in Espresso, to perform some
checks automatically, starting with Compose 1.8. To do so, add the UI test
junit4 accessibility library to use accessibility checks. To enable them, call
enable accessibility checks in the Android Compose test rule
and trigger an action, or try perform accessibility checks. This specific test fails with
an exception and a message that the item may not
have a label readable by accessibility services. Other checks can look for color
contrast, touch target size, or elements traversal order. In combination with
manual testing, automated tests that use
both Compose APIs, as well as the Accessibility
Test Framework, can help detect possible problems early
on in the development life cycle. We've covered a lot today,
from classifying composables for accessibility,
to understanding when and how to set
certain semantics, to exploring how to test
and debug components. We hope you've picked up
a few more tips on how to make your Compose
app more inclusive, and how to unlock delightful
experiences for even more users to enjoy. SIMONA MILANOVIC: Knowing when
to add just a quick content description to an image, or
a larger set of semantics for a custom
component, means you're helping accessibility services
better present your component, leading to a better
app experience for accessibility users. If you want to dive deeper
into Compose accessibility,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 80qkStdDWXQ
text:
for a custom
component, means you're helping accessibility services
better present your component, leading to a better
app experience for accessibility users. If you want to dive deeper
into Compose accessibility, take a look at our
newly added guidance. Link in the description. Thank you for being a part
of an ecosystem of highly accessible, inclusive apps. Happy coding! [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
